title: The Particle Model
subtitle: Solids, liquids and gases; changes of state; diffusion, gas pressure and expansion.

studyGuide.heading: The three states of matter
studyGuide.html:
Everything is made of tiny particles. How they are arranged decides the state.

  • Solid — particles touching, in a fixed regular pattern, vibrating on the spot. Fixed shape, fixed volume, cannot be compressed.
  • Liquid — particles touching but randomly arranged, able to slide past each other. Fixed volume, takes the shape of its container.
  • Gas — particles far apart, moving quickly in all directions. No fixed shape or volume; can be compressed.

diagram.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 360 130' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.b{fill:none;stroke:#1f2a44;stroke-width:2}.p{fill:#5b8def}.t{font:bold 12px Nunito;fill:#1f2a44}</style><rect class='b' x='15' y='20' width='80' height='80'/><g class='p'><circle cx='30' cy='35' r='6'/><circle cx='47' cy='35' r='6'/><circle cx='64' cy='35' r='6'/><circle cx='81' cy='35' r='6'/><circle cx='30' cy='52' r='6'/><circle cx='47' cy='52' r='6'/><circle cx='64' cy='52' r='6'/><circle cx='81' cy='52' r='6'/><circle cx='30' cy='69' r='6'/><circle cx='47' cy='69' r='6'/><circle cx='64' cy='69' r='6'/><circle cx='81' cy='69' r='6'/><circle cx='30' cy='86' r='6'/><circle cx='47' cy='86' r='6'/><circle cx='64' cy='86' r='6'/><circle cx='81' cy='86' r='6'/></g><text class='t' x='55' y='118' text-anchor='middle'>Solid</text><rect class='b' x='140' y='20' width='80' height='80'/><g class='p'><circle cx='155' cy='60' r='6'/><circle cx='172' cy='55' r='6'/><circle cx='190' cy='62' r='6'/><circle cx='206' cy='56' r='6'/><circle cx='160' cy='78' r='6'/><circle cx='180' cy='80' r='6'/><circle cx='200' cy='76' r='6'/><circle cx='168' cy='92' r='6'/><circle cx='190' cy='92' r='6'/></g><text class='t' x='180' y='118' text-anchor='middle'>Liquid</text><rect class='b' x='265' y='20' width='80' height='80'/><g class='p'><circle cx='278' cy='32' r='6'/><circle cx='320' cy='40' r='6'/><circle cx='300' cy='65' r='6'/><circle cx='335' cy='80' r='6'/><circle cx='275' cy='85' r='6'/></g><text class='t' x='305' y='118' text-anchor='middle'>Gas</text></svg>
diagram.caption: Solid: packed & regular. Liquid: packed & random. Gas: far apart & fast.

studyGuide.mnemonic: Going solid → liquid → gas, particles get more energy, move more and spread further apart.

studyGuide.heading: Changes of state
studyGuide.html:
• Melting — solid → liquid (at the melting point).
  • Freezing — liquid → solid (same temperature as melting).
  • Evaporating / boiling — liquid → gas.
  • Condensing — gas → liquid.
  • Subliming — solid → gas directly (e.g. dry ice).

Changes of state are physical changes — no new substance is made and they can be reversed. The mass stays the same.

diagram.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 360 90' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.b{fill:#eaf1fe;stroke:#5b8def;stroke-width:2}.t{font:bold 12px Nunito;fill:#1f2a44}.s{font:10px Nunito;fill:#2b9d6b}.r{font:10px Nunito;fill:#e0524d}</style><rect class='b' x='10' y='30' width='70' height='34' rx='8'/><text class='t' x='45' y='51' text-anchor='middle'>Solid</text><rect class='b' x='145' y='30' width='70' height='34' rx='8'/><text class='t' x='180' y='51' text-anchor='middle'>Liquid</text><rect class='b' x='280' y='30' width='70' height='34' rx='8'/><text class='t' x='315' y='51' text-anchor='middle'>Gas</text><text class='s' x='112' y='28' text-anchor='middle'>melt →</text><text class='r' x='112' y='78' text-anchor='middle'>← freeze</text><text class='s' x='247' y='28' text-anchor='middle'>boil →</text><text class='r' x='247' y='78' text-anchor='middle'>← condense</text></svg>
diagram.caption: Heating moves right (melt, boil); cooling moves left (condense, freeze).

studyGuide.heading: Heating, cooling and energy
studyGuide.html:
• Heating gives particles more kinetic energy so they move/vibrate faster.
  • During melting or boiling, the temperature stays constant even though heating continues — the energy is used to break the forces between particles, not to raise the temperature.
  • This gives the flat parts (plateaus) on a heating/cooling graph.

studyGuide.heading: Diffusion
studyGuide.html:
Diffusion is the spreading of particles from a region of high concentration to low concentration, until evenly spread.

  • Happens because particles move randomly.
  • Faster in gases (particles move fastest) and at higher temperatures.
  • Examples: smelling perfume across a room; a tea bag colouring water.
studyGuide.mnemonic: Diffusion spreads things out — particles diffuse from where there's a lot to where there's little.

studyGuide.heading: Gas pressure and expansion
studyGuide.html:
• Gas particles hit the walls of their container — these collisions cause gas pressure.
  • Heating a gas makes particles move faster and hit the walls harder and more often → higher pressure (or it expands).
  • Most materials expand when heated (particles vibrate more and take up more space) and contract when cooled.
  • This is why gaps are left in railway tracks and bridges.

flashcards.back: Touching, regular fixed pattern, vibrating on the spot.
Touching but random; can slide past each other.
Far apart, moving quickly in all directions.

flashcards.front: Why can't you compress a solid or liquid?
flashcards.back: The particles are already touching with no space between them.
Solid → liquid at the melting point.
Solid → gas directly, without becoming a liquid (e.g. dry ice).

flashcards.front: Is a change of state a new substance?
flashcards.back: No — it is a physical change, reversible, and mass is conserved.

flashcards.front: Why does temperature stay constant while melting?
flashcards.back: The energy is used to break the forces between particles, not to raise the temperature.
Particles spreading from high to low concentration until evenly mixed.
In gases and at higher temperatures (particles move faster).
Gas particles colliding with the walls of the container.

flashcards.front: Effect of heating a gas in a sealed container
flashcards.back: Particles move faster and hit the walls harder/more often, so pressure increases.

flashcards.front: Why do solids expand when heated?
flashcards.back: Particles vibrate more and take up slightly more space.

flashcards.front: Why are gaps left in railway tracks?
flashcards.back: To allow the metal to expand in hot weather without buckling.

flashcards.front: What happens to particle energy from solid to gas?
flashcards.back: It increases — particles gain kinetic energy and move more.

commonMistakes.title: Saying particles change size when heated
commonMistakes.detail: Particles do NOT get bigger. They move faster and further apart — the substance expands, not the particles.

commonMistakes.title: Thinking melting makes a new substance
commonMistakes.detail: Changes of state are physical and reversible — ice and water are the same substance.

commonMistakes.title: Saying mass changes when ice melts
commonMistakes.detail: Mass is conserved — the same particles are still there.

commonMistakes.title: Temperature rising during a state change
commonMistakes.detail: Temperature stays constant during melting/boiling while bonds break, giving a flat line on the graph.

commonMistakes.title: Confusing evaporating and boiling
commonMistakes.detail: Boiling happens throughout the liquid at the boiling point; evaporation happens only at the surface, at any temperature.

commonMistakes.title: Saying gases have no particles
commonMistakes.detail: Gases have the same particles, just far apart and fast-moving.

questions.q: In which state are the particles far apart and moving quickly?
questions.explanation: Gas particles are far apart and move fast in all directions.
questions.hint: Which one fills its container?

questions.q: Solid → liquid is called…
questions.hint: Like ice turning to water.

questions.q: Gas → liquid is called…
questions.hint: Like steam on a cold window.

questions.q: Why can a gas be compressed but a solid cannot?
options: Gas particles have big spaces between them
questions.explanation: Gas particles have large gaps that can be squeezed; solid particles are already touching.
questions.hint: Think about the spaces between particles.

questions.q: What is diffusion?
options: Particles spreading from high to low concentration
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the spreading of particles from high to low concentration.
questions.hint: Like a smell spreading across a room.

questions.q: When you heat a solid, the particles…
questions.explanation: Heating gives particles more energy so they vibrate faster (the particles do not change size).
questions.hint: More energy = more movement.

questions.q: Describe the arrangement and movement of particles in a solid.
questions.modelAnswer: In a solid the particles are packed closely together in a fixed, regular pattern and they vibrate on the spot. They cannot move from place to place, which is why a solid has a fixed shape.
questions.hint: Think arrangement (pattern) and movement (vibrate).
questions.examMistake: Saying solid particles do not move — they vibrate on the spot.

questions.q: Name the change of state when a gas turns straight into a solid or a solid straight into a gas.
questions.modelAnswer: Sublimation (subliming) — for example dry ice (solid carbon dioxide) turning straight into a gas.
questions.hint: It skips the liquid stage.
questions.examMistake: Saying 'evaporating' — that goes through a liquid stage.

questions.q: Which causes the pressure of a gas in a container?
questions.explanation: Gas pressure is caused by particles colliding with the walls.
questions.hint: What do the particles bang into?

questions.q: Liquid → solid is called…
questions.hint: Like water turning to ice.

questions.q: Why are gaps left between sections of a railway track?
questions.modelAnswer: When the metal gets hot it expands because the particles vibrate more and take up more space. The gaps give the metal room to expand without buckling or bending the track.
questions.hint: What happens to metal in hot weather?
questions.examMistake: Saying the particles get bigger — they just move more.

questions.q: A change of state such as melting is best described as…
options: A chemical change making a new substance
A physical change that can be reversed
questions.explanation: Changes of state are physical and reversible; no new substance forms.
questions.hint: Can you turn the water back into ice?

questions.q: Use the particle model to explain why a liquid takes the shape of its container but keeps the same volume.
questions.modelAnswer: In a liquid the particles are still touching but are arranged randomly and can slide past one another, so the liquid flows and takes the shape of its container. However, the particles are still close together with no large gaps, so the volume cannot change — the liquid keeps the same volume.
questions.hint: Particles can move (shape) but stay touching (volume).
questions.examMistake: Saying liquid particles are far apart — they are still touching.

questions.q: On a heating graph, the temperature stays flat while a solid melts. Why?
options: The energy is used to break forces between particles, not raise temperature
questions.explanation: During melting the supplied energy breaks the forces holding particles, so temperature stays constant.
questions.hint: Where is the energy going during melting?

questions.q: Explain why diffusion happens faster in a gas than in a liquid.
questions.modelAnswer: Diffusion happens because particles move randomly and spread out. In a gas the particles have much more energy, move much faster and have large spaces to move into, so they spread out and mix more quickly than in a liquid, where particles move more slowly and are packed closely together.
questions.hint: Compare particle speed and spacing in gas vs liquid.
questions.examMistake: Forgetting to compare both speed and spacing.

questions.q: A sealed syringe of gas is heated. What happens to the pressure if the volume can't change?
questions.explanation: Hotter particles move faster and hit the walls harder and more often, raising the pressure.
questions.hint: Faster particles hit the walls how?

questions.q: When ice melts, the mass of the water equals the mass of the ice. Explain why, using particles.
questions.modelAnswer: Melting is a physical change in which the same particles are still present — none are created or destroyed, they just gain energy and move apart slightly to become a liquid. Because the number and type of particles is unchanged, the mass stays exactly the same.
questions.hint: Are any particles added or removed when ice melts?
questions.examMistake: Thinking melting destroys or creates particles.

questions.q: Which statement about evaporation and boiling is correct?
options: Both happen only at the surface
Boiling happens throughout the liquid at the boiling point; evaporation happens at the surface at any temperature
Evaporation needs a higher temperature than boiling
questions.explanation: Boiling occurs throughout at the boiling point; evaporation is a surface effect at any temperature.
questions.hint: Where does each one happen in the liquid?

questions.q: Explain why a balloon shrinks when taken outside on a cold day.
questions.modelAnswer: In the cold the gas particles inside the balloon lose energy and move more slowly, so they hit the inside walls less often and with less force. The pressure inside drops and the outside air pressure pushes the balloon in, so it shrinks (the gas also contracts as it cools).
questions.hint: Cold = slower particles = weaker, fewer collisions.
questions.examMistake: Saying the particles shrink rather than slowing down and spreading less.

questions.q: A drop of food colouring spreads through still water without stirring. This is because…
options: Particles move randomly, so the colour diffuses
The colour is heavier than water
questions.explanation: Random particle movement causes the dye to diffuse through the water.
questions.hint: Diffusion needs no stirring.

questions.q: Describe what happens to the particles as a substance is cooled from a gas all the way to a solid.
questions.modelAnswer: As a gas cools, the particles lose energy and slow down. They come closer together and the gas condenses into a liquid, where particles touch but can still slide. On further cooling they lose more energy, move less, and arrange into a fixed regular pattern as the liquid freezes into a solid, where they only vibrate on the spot.
questions.hint: Follow energy decreasing: gas → liquid → solid.
questions.examMistake: Skipping the liquid stage or forgetting energy is being lost.

questions.q: Why does a hot air balloon rise?
options: Heated air expands and becomes less dense than the cooler air around it
questions.explanation: Heating spreads the particles out, making the air less dense so it floats upward.
questions.hint: Heating changes the density of the air.

questions.q: Sketch in words a heating curve for ice taken from -10°C to steam at 110°C, explaining the flat sections.
questions.modelAnswer: The temperature rises as the ice warms to 0°C. There is a flat section at 0°C while the ice melts — the energy breaks the forces between particles instead of raising temperature. The temperature then rises as the water warms to 100°C. A second, longer flat section occurs at 100°C while the water boils, again because energy is breaking the forces between particles. After all the water has become steam, the temperature rises again to 110°C.
questions.hint: Two plateaus: at the melting and boiling points.
questions.examMistake: Drawing the temperature rising during melting/boiling.

questions.q: Two gas jars, one of brown bromine gas and one of air, are placed mouth to mouth and the cover removed. After a while both jars are pale brown. Explain fully using particles.
questions.modelAnswer: The particles in both gases are moving randomly at high speed. The bromine particles spread from the bottom jar (high concentration) into the air jar (low concentration), while air particles move the other way. This diffusion continues until the bromine particles are evenly spread through both jars, so both look the same pale brown. No stirring is needed because the particles move on their own.
questions.hint: It's diffusion driven by random motion — describe the spread.
questions.examMistake: Forgetting that gas in the top jar also diffuses downward.

questions.q: Why does a substance often expand only a little when it melts, but expand a great deal when it boils?
options: Going to a gas spreads particles far apart, hugely increasing volume
questions.explanation: Solid and liquid particles both touch, so melting changes spacing little; becoming a gas spreads particles far apart, so volume increases enormously.
questions.hint: Compare the spacing change for melt vs boil.

questions.q: Explain why pressure inside a car tyre increases after a long, fast drive.
questions.modelAnswer: Driving fast makes the tyre heat up due to friction with the road. The air particles inside gain energy and move faster, so they collide with the inside walls of the tyre more often and with greater force. Since the volume of the tyre barely changes, this increase in collisions raises the gas pressure inside.
questions.hint: Hotter gas, fixed volume — what do collisions do?
questions.examMistake: Saying more air is added — it's the same air, just hotter.

questions.q: A student says 'when water boils, the bubbles are made of air'. Explain what is wrong and what the bubbles really are.
questions.modelAnswer: The bubbles are not air — they are water vapour (gaseous water). When water reaches its boiling point, particles inside the liquid gain enough energy to break free from each other and form gas, creating bubbles of water vapour that rise to the surface and escape. The substance is still water, just in the gas state.
questions.hint: What state is the water in inside the bubbles?
questions.examMistake: The very common belief that boiling bubbles are air.

questions.q: Metal lids on glass jars are easier to remove after running them under hot water. Why?
options: The metal expands more than the glass, loosening the lid
questions.explanation: Metal expands more than glass when heated, so the lid loosens slightly.
questions.hint: Different materials expand by different amounts.

questions.q: Explain why you can smell hot food from further away than cold food.
questions.modelAnswer: Smell is caused by particles from the food diffusing through the air to your nose. When the food is hot, its particles (and the air particles) have more energy and move faster, so they diffuse and spread out more quickly and reach further before becoming too dilute to detect — so you smell hot food from a greater distance.
questions.hint: Temperature affects how fast particles diffuse.
questions.examMistake: Not connecting temperature to particle speed and diffusion rate.

questions.q: Using the particle model, explain why solids and liquids cannot be squashed (compressed) but gases can.
questions.modelAnswer: In solids and liquids the particles are already touching each other with virtually no gaps between them, so there is no space to push them into — they cannot be compressed. In a gas the particles are far apart with large empty spaces between them, so applying pressure can push the particles closer together into those spaces, compressing the gas.
questions.hint: It's all about the spaces between particles.
questions.examMistake: Saying particles themselves get squashed — it's the spaces that close up.
